data.epi_content:
In the end, all the cookies I was eating were gone.

How did I endure that long time before?

Ah, I was just slowly dying because even eating was difficult.

Not like now. I wasn't full of positivity and hope like I am now!

Anyway, even though I'm immersed in optimism about the future, the fact that I need to go to the classroom soon is starting to loom over me, which is somewhat burdensome.

Well, Lydia will be waiting for me when I get there.

Imagining all sorts of creative, horrible things.

I need a breakthrough.

Something to navigate this situation.

Or perhaps giving in wouldn't be so bad either.

Reeds have no backbone, so when the wind blows, they simply bow and bend to avoid it.

If I stay trapped in this cage-like place forever, I'll eventually forget how to fly and become nothing more than a pet bird that can only flutter about.

Or even if I remember how to fly, someone might come along and cut off all my feathers.

But when placed alone in the world, unable to bow even when I want to, what exactly should I do?

When I was thinking about dying, I only had to focus on that one thing, and just act toward that goal, which was quite simple.

But now that I'm trying to think about doing something for myself, I suddenly feel suffocated.

"And yet you were begging to be saved before."

This useless stubbornness and obstinacy won't leave my body.

Speaking politely, not cursing, always acting strangely normal no matter how much my body hurts.

Perhaps bringing a blade to my skin and scratching it slightly is not only changing the uncertainty of my existence in this world into something real, but also shedding those aspects of this young lady.

But it's neither this young lady nor me.

Does the distinction even matter?

I'm sitting here wasting time, claiming to be mentally preparing myself, but I'm really just hiding here because I'm afraid of the classroom where Lydia is.

So, I opened the door and left the room.

Since it's not morning, I don't see children going to class, but this corridor I haven't been in for a long time gives me a fresh feeling.

After breathing the outside air, I understand why Vivian and that impudent maid made those expressions when they entered my room.

When you're breathing in a normal place like this, the blood scent floating in the air, the sticky dried rotten blood smell, and the strangely mixed chocolate scent are enough to turn your stomach.

"How refreshing."

To others, I might look the same as always, but I take what feels to me like light steps toward the classroom.

And in the classroom, the lesson had already ended with the teacher just keeping their seat, students who study were pretending to study, and the rest were gathered chatting or playing.

Then, when I entered the classroom, everyone looked at me.

But I didn't care and just sat down in an empty seat.

The teacher saw my face, checked attendance, and then left the classroom.

The teacher could only end the class after checking through magic that all the students were present.

Otherwise, someone like me before my family fell could intimidate the teacher and demand to be marked present without actually coming to class.

Well, attendance might keep being checked even when you're not actually in the capital.

Anyway, it must have been quite a burden since I hadn't been showing up for a while.

And, of course, when I enter the classroom, Lydia comes looking for me.

As if all conversations had already ended, she blocks my path so I can't escape.

"Miss Erica Mecklenburg, it's been a while."

"...Yes, it has been."

"You must have been quite heartbroken? Duke Mecklenburg, pfft. I don't know who would believe it, but anyway, died in an accident, and your brother ended up in that state too."
data.epi_content2:
It's getting tiresome at this point.

If a tragic and miserable event happens only once, it hits quite shockingly.

When it comes a second time, you start to wonder if it's your fate.

From the third time, it seems to get tiresome.

Even if I die, I can't really die, so I can't even say I'm tired of living.

Actually, such thoughts do come to mind.

Every time I die, time rewinds a little bit, whether it's 30 minutes or an hour.

So, if I keep dying, wouldn't something change?

But at the point where I can do such things, I'd become a completely insane person with no possibility of rehabilitation.

A person who becomes numb to pain, whose senses and emotions gradually disappear and are erased, can't be called human anymore.

"I suppose so."

So, I recall the sadness.

Even though I've heard and experienced this many times, I somehow pull out the edge of sadness from my heart to evoke a melancholic feeling.

Feeling as if my eyes are getting slightly red, I don't show it outwardly.

Before, my expression would have almost crumbled.

Now, I just pass through it indifferently.

"You must be heartbroken."

"Hmm, that's not the reaction I was expecting."

Lydia looks at me with an expressionless face that only a robot could make, appreciating me from different angles as if looking at a product.

She makes a "hmm" sound while supporting her chin with her finger.

"Well, it doesn't matter."

Then she puts on a bright, emotionless face again.

No, maybe it's a happy expression.

"Anyway, if you're so sad, how about playing a fun game with me?

The best way to shake off sadness is through amusement!"

I look around.

The children who usually study were pretending to study something diligently while glancing at us.

The children surrounding me, preventing me from escaping, were busy whispering to each other and giggling.

And Lydia in front of me, I don't know what she was thinking, but she looked happy anyway.

When I nodded, Lydia said with a bright face as if pleased.

Her face was bright, but her eyes were not.

"You've thought well!

I like good and obedient puppies like me!

Now that our positions seem to have changed a bit, it seems it's your turn to be the cute puppy, Miss."

Lydia moves away from me to her seat, then rummages through her bag.

Sticking out her tongue, making a confused expression, and then exaggeratedly searching through her bag—is it to instill fear in me?

Or is it just for mockery?

Fortunately, she wasn't carrying a dog leash; what she took out of her bag was a headband with animal ears.

"Would you like to try it on?"

Lydia didn't forcibly put the headband on me, she just handed it over.

As if telling me to take it and wear it myself.

I took the headband and just placed it on top of my head.

"Miss, it's a gift.
data.epi_content3:
Shouldn't it be accompanied by thanks and a smile?"

"...Thank you."

"As for the smile, well, let's skip that!

I've never seen you smile before, so maybe this is you smiling right now.

Asking a charmless girl who doesn't know how to smile to smile.

It might be too cruel, I'm sorry!"

I wondered why she was saying such nonsense.

"Well, who would like a high-handed young lady who doesn't even know how to smile?"

She delivered one heart-sinking comment right to the mark.

"I wonder if high nobles who lead the country are really different.

It's my first time bullying someone who was once higher than me, so I'm not sure."

Lydia says this and raises her hand.

"When I bully mediocre people, most of them react with 'How dare you!' or 'How could you..!'

But you, Miss, know your place and are very obedient... heh."

I thought she was going to slap my cheek, but when she put her hand on my head and tried to pat it, I unconsciously knocked her hand away.

At the same time, Lydia's expression froze coldly, looking down at me with contemptuous eyes.

Her eyes were cold.

Not looking at a person as a person, but as something to be dealt with.

Yes, it's similar to the expression the old man standing next to the boy at the execution ground had when looking at me.

Perhaps Lydia's ancestor might have been an executioner who diligently cut off people's heads.

Lydia touched her face once, then relaxed her expression and spoke with a broad smile as if talking to a close friend.

"I guess not always.

Well, that's enough for today! See you tomorrow, Miss. Hee."

Lydia led the children out of the classroom.

Only then did I mutter a complaint.

"I'd rather not see you again."

I took off the headband and threw it away.

The children who were pretending not to look but were actually glancing at me flinched.

Not wanting to stay in the classroom any longer, I got up and returned to my room.

People who knew my face looked slightly surprised when they saw me and just passed by.

Because I'm no longer a person worth maintaining a relationship with.

It's something I can't get used to, just like Evan verbally stabbing me and comparing me to Vivian, thinking I'm not better in any way, and Lydia bullying me.

Anyway, if I keep bending like this, it will end someday.

It was a bit humiliating, but they didn't lock me in a locker like last time, so maybe it's okay.

I looked in the mirror.

Can't smile? That can't be right.

I tried to smile, but only the corners of my mouth twitched awkwardly.

Like in that movie I saw before with a psychopath protagonist, I hooked my fingers in my mouth and pulled the corners up.

It hurt, as if the muscles had stiffened.

Anyway, I cried because it hurt.
